title: Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes in Robotics: A Survey
authors: Mikko Lauri; David Hsu; Joni Pajarinen
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2209.10342 on 21 Sep 2022
open_source_notes: No single companion implementation is associated with this broad survey.
primary_problem: Survey; POMDP Planning; Decision Making Under Uncertainty
long_horizon: N/A — finite-, infinite-, and receding-horizon methods are reviewed
human_robot_interaction: N/A — HRI is one reviewed application area
uses_world_model: N/A — reviews explicit and learned transition, observation, and reward models
world_model_family: N/A — probabilistic state-transition and observation models
world_model_detail: Reviews POMDP formulations that maintain belief states and reason using transition, observation, and reward models under partial observability.
uses_planning: N/A — offline, online, sampling-based, and heuristic POMDP planners are reviewed
horizon_basis: The survey covers finite-horizon, infinite-horizon, online receding-horizon, and temporally abstract planning.
observation_modality: Multiple; potentially continuous and high dimensional
action_space: Multiple; discrete and continuous approaches reviewed
benchmark: Applications across navigation, driving, search and tracking, manipulation, HRI, and multi-robot coordination
main_contribution: Connects POMDP theory and algorithms to practical modeling choices across six major robotics application areas.
novelty: Provides a robotics-centered synthesis of when POMDP structure is useful and how task characteristics affect model and solver selection.
limitations: POMDP deployment remains constrained by computational complexity, continuous and high-dimensional spaces, model acquisition, and gaps between assumed models and real systems.
future_work: Integrate model learning and planning more tightly, improve continuous-space and high-dimensional methods, and scale constrained POMDPs for safe robotics.
evidence_world_model: POMDP planning explicitly reasons through transition, observation, and reward models, which may be specified or learned, but this survey proposes no single world model.
evidence_planning: The paper reviews exact, approximate, offline, online, Monte Carlo, and receding-horizon solution algorithms over belief states.
evidence_horizon: Both finite and infinite horizons, long-horizon abstraction, and online limited-horizon approximations are reviewed.
review_flags: Foundational planning-under-uncertainty survey; retain for theoretical framing but exclude from method and architecture counts.
source_document: Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes in Robotics_ A Survey.txt

title: PIVOT-R: Primitive-Driven Waypoint-Aware World Model for Robotic Manipulation
authors: Kaidong Zhang; Pengzhen Ren; Bingqian Lin; Junfan Lin; Shikui Ma; Hang Xu; Xiaodan Liang
year_basis: First public appearance: arXiv:2410.10394 in Oct 2024; published at NeurIPS 2024
venue: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS)
open_source_notes: A project website is provided, but a public code release was not established from the supplied manuscript.
primary_problem: World Modeling; Policy Learning; Waypoint Prediction; Efficient Execution
world_model_detail: A VLM parses one of ten manipulation primitives, and a scene-prediction transformer predicts the CLIP feature of a task-relevant future waypoint for conditioning a lightweight action decoder.
action_conditioned: Yes — conditioned on current observations, robot state, action history, and primitive prompt
planning_method: None — primitive parsing and waypoint prediction feed a direct action policy without search or candidate rollout evaluation
horizon_basis: The method predicts the next task-relevant waypoint and low-level action; SeaWave levels measure instruction complexity rather than temporal horizon.
hierarchical_planning: No — modules run at different frequencies, but no separate task-level plan is optimized
replanning_online: Yes — primitive, waypoint, and action predictions are refreshed asynchronously from current observations
learning_type: Imitation Learning; Latent Waypoint Prediction; Vision-Language Pretraining
pretraining: Yes — pretrained VLM and CLIP encoders
observation_modality: First- and third-person RGB; language; robot state; action history
goal_representation: Natural-language instruction parsed into manipulation primitives
action_space: Discretized delta position, delta Euler rotation, and binary gripper state
benchmark: SeaWave four-level instruction benchmark; three custom real-world tasks
robot_platform: SeaWave simulated manipulator; real robot platform not specified in the extracted manuscript text
main_contribution: Predicts sparse task-relevant visual waypoints rather than modeling every timestep, improving manipulation accuracy and execution efficiency.
novelty: Combines primitive-guided latent waypoint prediction with an asynchronous hierarchy whose modules run at different frequencies.
limitations: High-level primitive instructions and executed low-level actions can disagree, particularly for directional commands, and large action modules may overfit.
future_work: Strengthen consistency between high-level instructions and low-level execution so actions can adapt reliably to requested changes.
evidence_world_model: The scene module predicts the latent CLIP representation of a future waypoint state from current multimodal context.
evidence_planning: No search, MPC, or multi-candidate rollout stage is used; the predicted waypoint directly conditions action decoding.
evidence_horizon: The benchmark contains individual manipulation skills with progressively harder language grounding, not long-horizon multi-stage tasks.
review_flags: Core predictive waypoint world model, but exclude from explicit-planner counts. Do not interpret SeaWave Level 4 as long-horizon.
source_document: PIVOT-R_ Primitive-Driven Waypoint-Aware World Model for Robotic Manipulation.txt

title: Planning Single-Arm Manipulations with N-Arm Robots
authors: Benjamin Cohen; Mike Phillips; Maxim Likhachev
year_basis: Original paper appeared at Robotics: Science and Systems 2014; supplied file is a short version published at SoCS 2015
publication_status: Peer-reviewed conference article; short version in SoCS 2015
open_source_notes: No code release was identified in the supplied short paper.
task_domain: Multi-Arm Manipulation; Object Transport; Handover Planning
world_model_detail: N/A — the system uses an explicit kinematic and collision model.
planning_representation: Hybrid graph over object pose, supporting arm, grasp, and manipulator redundancy parameters
horizon_basis: Each problem moves one object from a start to a goal pose and typically requires roughly one to two handoffs.
hierarchical_planning: No — discrete arm/grasp choices and continuous motion are handled within one search representation
learning_type: None — heuristic search and motion planning
observation_modality: Known object pose, obstacles, robot kinematics, and grasp set
action_space: Object-pose motion primitives, free-angle changes, grasp changes, and arm handoffs
benchmark: Four planning scenes: two-arm tabletop, three-arm bar, three-arm checkout, and four-arm factory
robot_platform: Two to four simulated PR2 7-DoF arms
main_contribution: Plans object transport across an arbitrary number of overlapping robot arms while jointly selecting arm order, grasps, handoff locations, and collision-free motions.
novelty: Searches primarily in the object's six-dimensional pose while tracking only the currently supporting arm, avoiding the full joint configuration space of all arms.
limitations: The method assumes unused arms remain in predefined safe configurations, evaluates only one transported object, and shows an 80% success rate across 25 simulated trials.
future_work: Not explicitly stated in the supplied short paper.
evidence_world_model: The planner uses deterministic kinematics, inverse kinematics, and collision checking rather than learned dynamics.
evidence_planning: Lazy Weighted A* jointly searches object motions, arm support changes, grasp choices, and handoff feasibility.
evidence_horizon: The task is a single object relocation with an average of approximately 1.3–2 handoffs, rather than a long task sequence.
review_flags: Historical multi-arm manipulation-planning baseline; record year as 2014 under the original-publication rule.
source_document: Planning Single-Arm Manipulations with N-Arm Robots.txt

title: Real-world robot applications of foundation models: a review
authors: Kento Kawaharazuka; Tatsuya Matsushima; Andrew Gambardella; Jiaxian Guo; Chris Paxton; Andy Zeng
open_source_notes: The article is open access, but no single companion code repository is associated with the review.
task_domain: General Robotics; Foundation Models; Real-World Deployment
primary_problem: Survey; Foundation Models; Perception; Planning; Control
long_horizon: N/A — reviews high-level and low-level planning applications
language_conditioned: N/A — many reviewed systems use language
human_robot_interaction: N/A — human interaction and communication applications are reviewed
uses_world_model: N/A — generative and multimodal foundation models are reviewed among broader model classes
world_model_family: N/A — multiple foundation-model and generative-model families
world_model_detail: Organizes foundation models by modality and input-output mapping, then studies their replacement of perception, planning, control, and data-generation components.
uses_planning: N/A — high-level planning, code generation, and low-level planning applications are reviewed
planning_representation: Multiple: language, code, latent features, images, actions, and skills
horizon_basis: The review discusses hierarchical skills and long-horizon code or task planning but proposes no single planner.
observation_modality: Language; vision; audio; 3D; force and other modalities
benchmark: Multiple real-world robot studies and robotic foundation-model datasets
main_contribution: Surveys how foundation models can replace or augment specific components in practical robot systems, from perception through planning and control.
novelty: Uses input-output modality transformations and robot-stack component replacement as its organizing framework, with emphasis on real-world deployment.
limitations: The field still underuses force, depth, inertial, and motion modalities; control rates are slow; low-level force control, safety, outdoor deployment, hardware diversity, and fair real-world evaluation remain limited.
future_work: Develop hierarchical skill interfaces, faster and safer embodied foundation models, richer multimodal sensing, broader hardware and outdoor deployment, stronger open-world generalization, and real-world evaluation standards.
evidence_world_model: Generative and multimodal models are reviewed, but the paper does not propose one predictive environment model.
evidence_planning: The survey separately reviews high-level task planning, code generation, low-level planning, control, and data augmentation.
evidence_horizon: It identifies skill hierarchy and planning granularity as future challenges rather than evaluating a paper-specific horizon.
review_flags: Supporting survey for foundation-model deployment and component-level analysis; exclude from method-only counts.
source_document: Real-world robot applications of foundation models  a review.txt

title: Research status of elderly-care robots and safe human-robot interaction methods
authors: Donghui Zhao; Xingwang Sun; Bo Shan; Zihao Yang; Junyou Yang; Houde Liu; Yinlai Jiang; Yokoi Hiroshi
open_source_notes: The article is open access under CC BY; no companion software resource is associated with the review.
task_domain: Elderly-Care Robotics; Assistive Robotics; Human-Robot Interaction
primary_problem: Survey; Safe Human-Robot Interaction; Assistive Robotics
human_robot_interaction: N/A — safe interaction is the survey's central topic
uses_world_model: N/A — not focused on learned predictive world models
world_model_detail: Reviews sensing, prediction, control, planning, biomechanics, and social factors for safe elderly-care interaction.
uses_planning: N/A — motion planning and prediction are reviewed as safety mechanisms
horizon_basis: No single planning architecture or normalized horizon is evaluated.
observation_modality: Multiple: physiological sensors, vision, wearable sensing, force, motion, and smart-home sensors
goal_representation: Multiple care, rehabilitation, monitoring, and companionship objectives
benchmark: Multiple elderly-care robot systems, rehabilitation devices, companion robots, and safe-HRI studies
main_contribution: Reviews elderly-care robot categories, nursing modes, and the technical and social methods needed for safe interaction with frail users.
novelty: Integrates robot-care functions with physical safety, intention understanding, biomechanics, ethics, psychology, and user acceptance.
limitations: Current systems remain specialized, rely heavily on professional supervision, have limited flexibility and autonomy, and face safety, ethical, privacy, and acceptance barriers.
future_work: Coordinate heterogeneous assistive robots, build robot-and-sensor smart-home care models, improve mechanical flexibility and safety, and increase user acceptance.
evidence_world_model: The review includes prediction and sensing methods for safety but is not organized around learned action-conditioned environment models.
evidence_planning: Motion planning and prediction are discussed as components of safe interaction rather than as one proposed planner.
evidence_horizon: The paper provides no paper-level planning horizon.
review_flags: Peripheral to the core world-model and long-horizon manipulation corpus; retain only for HRI and safety context.
source_document: Research status of elderly-care robots and safe human-robot interaction methods.txt
